md: Two triangles can have the same shape but different sizes, the same shape *and* the same size, or be totally different. The first situation is called **similarity**; the second is **congruence**. Both ideas show up everywhere — in proofs, in scaling drawings, in solving real-world problems with proportions.

blocks.title: Definitions
blocks.body:
**Congruent** triangles: same shape AND same size. All corresponding sides equal, all corresponding angles equal. Symbol: $\cong$.
**Similar** triangles: same shape, sizes can differ. Corresponding angles equal, corresponding sides *proportional*. Symbol: $\sim$.

md: You don't need to verify all six measurements (3 sides + 3 angles) to prove congruence. Any of these four 'shortcuts' is enough:
**SSS** (Side-Side-Side): all three pairs of sides equal.
**SAS** (Side-Angle-Side): two sides and the *included* angle (between them) equal.
**ASA** (Angle-Side-Angle): two angles and the *included* side equal.
**AAS** (Angle-Angle-Side): two angles and a non-included side equal.

blocks.title: Watch out — SSA doesn't work
blocks.body: Two sides and a *non-included* angle (SSA) is **not** enough to guarantee congruence. There can be two different triangles satisfying the same SSA setup. SSA is sometimes called the 'ambiguous case.'

md: Once two triangles are proved congruent, all corresponding parts are equal. This shorthand is **CPCTC**: *Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are Congruent*. It's a classic chain of reasoning in proofs: prove congruent first by SSS/SAS/ASA/AAS, then use CPCTC to claim any other matching parts are equal.

Two triangles are similar if just **two pairs of angles are equal**. Why two? Because the third pair must also be equal — the angles of a triangle sum to 180°, so if two pairs match, the third is forced.
Two other similarity tests: **SAS similarity** (two sides proportional and the included angle equal) and **SSS similarity** (all three sides proportional).
When two triangles are similar with scale factor $k$, every length in one is $k$ times the corresponding length in the other. That includes sides, but also altitudes, medians, perimeters, and so on.
**Areas scale by $k^2$.** This is why doubling the dimensions of a triangle multiplies its area by 4, not 2.

blocks.tex: \dfrac{\text{side in big}}{\text{corresponding side in small}} = k \qquad \dfrac{\text{area of big}}{\text{area of small}} = k^2

md: Two similar triangles have a scale factor of 3. The smaller triangle has sides $4, 5, 6$. What are the sides of the bigger one?
Multiply each side by 3: $12, 15, 18$. The big triangle's perimeter is also 3× bigger; its area is $3^2 = 9$× bigger.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Congruent**: same shape AND size. Prove with SSS, SAS, ASA, or AAS — never SSA. Then use **CPCTC** for the rest of the parts. **Similar**: same shape, possibly different size. Prove with AA, SAS sim, or SSS sim. Sides scale by $k$, areas scale by $k^2$.
